questions.clue: This network of secret routes and safe houses, organized primarily by free and enslaved African Americans, helped more than 100,000 enslaved people escape to freedom in the Northern United States and Canada before the Civil War.

citations.title: Underground Railroad
citations.snippet: The Underground Railroad was an organized network of secret routes and safe houses for fugitive slaves to escape to the abolitionist Northern United States and Eastern Canada during the era of slavery in the United States. Slaves escaped fr

questions.clue: The assassination of this Austro-Hungarian heir to the throne by Gavrilo Princip in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914 set off the chain of mobilizations and declarations of war that ignited the First World War.

citations.title: World War I
citations.snippet: World War I, or the First World War, also known as the Great War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Central Powers. Major areas of conflict included Europe and the Middle East, as well as parts of Africa and t

questions.clue: On February 20, 1962, this American astronaut circled the Earth three times aboard Friendship 7, becoming the first American to orbit the planet; he later flew again at age 77, making him the oldest person to reach orbit.

citations.title: John Glenn
citations.snippet: John Herschel Glenn Jr. was an American Marine Corps aviator, astronaut, businessman, and politician. He was the third American in space and the first to orbit the Earth, circling it three times in 1962. Following his retirement from NASA,

questions.clue: Styling himself 'the Sun King,' this French monarch held the throne for 72 years and 110 days — the longest reign of any sovereign in recorded history — and made the Palace of Versailles his royal seat to consolidate absolute power.

citations.title: Louis XIV
citations.snippet: Louis XIV was King of France from 14 May 1643 until his death in 1715. He is a symbol of the Age of Absolutism in Europe for styling himself as "The Sun King" and "Louis the Great", which portrayed him as supreme leader. He presided over a

questions.clue: This Carthaginian general invaded Italy in 218 BC by leading North African war elephants across the Alps and inflicted a devastating defeat on Rome at the Battle of Cannae in 216 BC, killing an estimated 50,000 Roman soldiers in a single day.

questions.clue: While imprisoned in 1946, this American civil rights leader and Muslim minister joined the Nation of Islam, replacing his birth surname 'Little' with a symbol of his unknown African ancestral name and discarding what he called his 'white slavemaster name.'

questions.clue: In his 1961 farewell address, this Republican president coined the phrase 'military–industrial complex' to warn Americans against the growing political influence of the defense industry on government policy.

citations.title: Dwight D. Eisenhower

questions.clue: This 18th-century British nobleman allegedly demanded sliced meat between bread so he could eat without leaving his card game; his aristocratic title has since become the name of one of the world's most widely eaten foods.

questions.clue: One of the first U.S. Army Air Forces B-17 heavy bombers to complete 25 combat missions over Europe, this aircraft and its crew returned home to sell war bonds and inspired both a 1944 documentary and a 1990 Hollywood feature film sharing its name.

questions.clue: This deliberate famine of the early 1930s killed millions in Soviet Ukraine through enforced grain collection quotas and forcible collectivization, and it remains central to that country's national identity and its case for recognition as a genocide.

questions.clue: Louis XIV's 1685 revocation of this 1598 proclamation abolished the rights of France's Huguenot Protestant minority, subjected them to a wave of dragonnades and forced conversion, and drove hundreds of thousands into exile across Europe.

questions.clue: This Bavarian capital served as the birthplace and early headquarters of the Nazi Party in the 1920s, earning it the official designation 'Capital of the Movement,' before being heavily damaged during Allied bombing campaigns in World War II.

citations.title: Munich
citations.snippet: Munich is the capital and most populous city of Bavaria, Germany. As of 30 November 2024, its population was 1,604,384, making it the third-largest city in Germany after Berlin and Hamburg. Munich is the largest city in Germany that is not

questions.clue: Historians regard this nearly 900-day blockade of a major Soviet city as the deadliest military encirclement in history; when it ended in January 1944, the city — renamed in 1924 after the Bolshevik leader who had died that year — had lost over one million civilians.

citations.title: Saint Petersburg

questions.clue: Named after a Roman dictator nicknamed 'the Delayer,' this war-of-attrition doctrine — applied against the Carthaginian invader of Italy by avoiding pitched battle and instead harassing supply lines — remains a key concept in military history.

questions.clue: The largest and most powerful realm in Europe during the 10th and 11th centuries, this early medieval East Slavic state emerged in the 9th century and is regarded as the forerunner of modern Ukraine, Russia, and Belarus before being destroyed by the Mongols in the 13th century.
